ITER VV Lateral Rattle — Research Findings

MC n=5 000 · scipy HiGHS LP · imas-ink-repl · 2026-05-27

imas-ink serving fixed. Plots generated via imas-ink-repl (persistent matplotlib Python REPL) and served via imas-ink-serve_chart (SSE push to browser). GIFs animated in-browser — no external files needed.

1 — Correct LP Rattle Numbers

QuantityValueNotes
Max VV centre displacement ±X±1.547 mmLP bound, nominal assembly (u=0)
Max VV centre displacement ±Y±1.523 mmLP bound, nominal assembly
Max VV rotation θ±0.1875 mradAnalytic: δ/R = 1.5mm/8m
Displacement at R=3m (inner equip.)≤ 2.11 mm1.547 + 0.1875e-3×3000 mm
MC rattle P95 (any direction)2.282 mm5000 samples, independently sampled gaps
MC rattle P992.556 mm
MC rattle worst observed2.896 mmFrom 5000 samples
Absolute theoretical maximum3.094 mm2 × 1.547mm — see §2

2 — Why Can Rattle Exceed 3 mm?

The 3mm total gap is the slot width at one support in the toroidal direction. The VV has three rigid-body DOFs: translation (dx, dy) and rotation (dθ). The LP exploits rotation coupling: to move the VV centre +1.547mm in X, it simultaneously rotates by +0.193mrad. This rotation reduces the toroidal load on the critical support (top, φ=90°) from 1.547mm to ~0mm, while the bottom supports reach the 1.5mm limit. Without rotation, pure X translation of 1.547mm would demand 1.547mm toroidal displacement at the top support — violating the 1.5mm gap.

The rattle range from one extreme to the other is therefore:
  −1.547mm → +1.547mm = 3.094mm
This exceeds 3mm because the rotation allows the VV centre to displace slightly more than the raw gap width at any single support.

The rattle from a given assembly state in one direction can also exceed 1.547mm when the assembly state pre-loads certain supports in the "helpful" direction, leaving more than 1.5mm of forward gap at the binding supports. MC shows P95 of +X rattle alone = 1.830mm.

3 — LP Explained

LP = Linear Programme — an optimisation problem where both the objective function and all constraints are linear in the unknowns. Solved in microseconds by specialised solvers (we use scipy.optimize.linprog with the HiGHS backend).

For one assembly state u (9 known pin positions in mm), the rattle LP in direction θ is:

Variables: δq = (δdx, δdy, δdθ)   — additional rigid-body motion

Objective:     maximise   cos(θ)·δdx + sin(θ)·δdy        [mm translation in direction θ]

Constraints:    A·δq  ≤  1.5mm − u    [can't overshoot + side of gap]
               −A·δq  ≤  1.5mm + u    [can't overshoot − side of gap]

where A[i,:] = [−sin φᵢ,  cos φᵢ,  R=8000mm]   (kinematic matrix, 9×3)

We run this LP for 48 directions θ. The worst-case direction gives the rattle magnitude. For the full MC: 5000 assembly states × 48 LP solves = 240 000 LP problems, completing in ~4 minutes.

Why not pseudoinverse? The pseudoinverse computes q₀ = A⁺u (best-fit rigid-body state) and then estimates rattle analytically. But the analytical formula assumes u ∈ Col(A) — i.e., the gaps are consistent with some rigid-body motion. Independently-sampled gaps are never in Col(A). The pseudoinverse overestimates rattle by 24% (1.920mm vs 1.547mm) and catastrophically fails for the alternating case (predicts 2.253mm instead of correct 0mm).

4 — Principal Axis of Motion

You are correct that each assembly state has a principal axis — the direction of maximum rattle. The rattle envelope is a convex polytope (not a circle); it has a well-defined major axis.

For the nominal assembly, the principal axis is at ~±35° from X (determined by the 9-support geometry). With 9 equally-spaced supports, the stiffness is nearly isotropic (small anisotropy from the discrete spacing). The nominal LP gives X=1.547mm vs Y=1.523mm — a 1.6% asymmetry.

For arbitrary assembly states, the principal axis can rotate significantly — it depends on which supports have more remaining gap in which direction. The animated GIFs below show the principal-axis motion for different assembly states.

5 — Scriptable VV Visualisation

The plot_vv_state(ax, q, u) function is now in the imas-ink REPL namespace. It accepts:

plot_vv_state(ax, q_mm_mrad=(dx, dy, dtheta),
              u_assembly_mm=[u1,...,u9],
              rattle_envelope_mm=None)   # optional Nx2 array of δ(dx,dy) points

Elements: VV ring (thick blue circle centred at (dx,dy)), machine axis (+), 9 support slots (grey bars ±1.5mm scale), pin position dots (red=+toroidal, blue=−toroidal), rattle envelope overlay (orange).

6 — Animated Rattle GIFs

Each animation shows the VV ring moving along the principal rattle axis from its assembly position, with pins sliding within their slots. The orange envelope shows the full reachable region. Generated via matplotlib.animation + pillow writer in the imas-ink REPL.

Nominal assembly (all gaps centred) — max rattle 1.547mm
Pins move symmetrically; VV translates + rotates

Worst-case MC assembly — max rattle 2.896mm
Several pins pre-loaded leaving large gaps in rattle direction

7 — Gap Measurement Strategy

Full measurement (all 9 supports)

Knowing all uᵢ after installation: solve one LP per direction → exact deterministic rattle envelope. No MC needed. Measurement accuracy of ±0.1mm per gap propagates to only ±0.06mm rattle uncertainty.

Partial measurement (e.g. 4 of 9 supports)

If supports {i₁,...,i₄} are measured and the rest are unknown:

MC approach:
  for each trial:
      u_known[i₁,...,i₄] = measured values    (fixed)
      u_unknown[i₅,...,i₉] ~ Uniform(±1.5mm) (sampled)
      rattle = LP(u_full)
  → distribution tighter than full MC

The more supports measured, the tighter the rattle distribution. A "worst-case given measurements" bound can be obtained by optimising the unknown gaps to maximise rattle — this is a single LP over the 3 motion DOFs plus the 5 unknown gap variables.

Practically: measuring the 2–3 supports closest to the binding direction (supports 4 and 5 for X-motion) gives the largest reduction in uncertainty for X-rattle.

  4. Displacement envelope at attachment radii R=1, 3, 5m
  5. Assembly team deliverable (standalone HTML, clean)
